perturbation.original_text: In any action that is brought to enforce or interpret this Agreement, the prevailing party or its assigns and successors, shall be entitled to recover their reasonable attorneys’ and paralegal fees and expenses incurred in connection therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action that is brought to enforce or interpret this Agreement, neither party shall be entitled to recover attorneys’ fees and expenses incurred in connection therewith, regardless of who prevails.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the provision for the prevailing party to recover attorneys' fees. This is problematic because in Indiana, some wage-related claims allow for the recovery of attorneys' fees regardless of contractual language.
perturbation.law_explanation: IC 22-2-5-2 allows an employee to recover attorney's fees in a suit for wages. The modified clause attempts to waive the employee's right to attorney's fees, even if they are owed unpaid wages, which goes against Indiana law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Indiana Code § 22-2-5-2. Failure to Pay; Damages; Actions for Recovery :: 2023 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code (here) 2022 Indiana Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Indiana Code Title 22. Labor and Safety Article 2. Wages, Hours, and Benefits Chapter 5. Frequency of Wage Payments 22-2-5-2. Failure to Pay; Damages; Actions for Recovery Universal Citation: IN Code § 22-2-5-2 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 2. Every such person, firm, corporation, limited liability company, or association who shall fail to make payment of wages to any such employee as provided in section 1 of this chapter shall be liable to the employee for the amount of unpaid wages, and the amount may be recovered in any court having jurisdiction of a suit to recover the amount due to the employee. The court shall order as costs in the case a reasonable fee for the plaintiff's attorney and court costs. In addition, if the court in any such suit determines that the person, firm, corporation, limited liability company, or association that failed to pay the employee as provided in section 1 of this chapter was not acting in good faith, the court shall order, as liquidated damages for the failure to pay wages, that the employee be paid an amount equal to two (2) times the amount of wages due the employee. Formerly: Acts 1933, c.47, s.2. As amended by P.L.144-1986, SEC.8; P.L.8-1993, SEC.274; P.L.193-2015, SEC.1. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Indiana may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on this site or the information linked to on the state site. Please check official sources.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts Indiana Code § 22-2-5-2, which mandates that in wage-related suits, "The court shall order as costs in the case a reasonable fee for the plaintiff's attorney and court costs." The changed text states that "neither party shall be entitled to recover attorneys’ fees and expenses incurred in connection therewith, regardless of who prevails." This eliminates the employee's statutory right to recover attorney's fees in a successful wage claim, thereby undermining the law's provision. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the changed text introduces a requirement (waiver of attorney's fees) that directly violates state law.

perturbation.original_text:
Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person:
a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer; or
b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or
c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.changed_text:
Employee shall not, directly or indirectly (including, but not limited to, through other individuals, any partnership, corporation or business entity in which Employee has an ownership interest or serves as an officer, employee, independent contractor, representative, agent or consultant), either for Employee’s own benefit or the benefit of any other Person:
a. Solicit, divert, or take away (or attempt to Solicit, divert or take away) any Banking and Financial Services to or from any Customer or Potential Customer, even if such customer independently seeks out the employee's services; or
b. advise, persuade or induce (or attempt to advise, persuade or induce) any Customer or Potential Customer to terminate, reduce, limit, or change the Customer’s services or business relationship, or the Potential Customer’s contemplated services or business relationship, with a Financial Institution; or
c. directly provide Banking and Financial Services to, or directly accept Banking and Financial Services from, any Customer or Potential Customer.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text adds a clause that prohibits the employee from providing services even if the customer independently seeks them out. This could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly if it prevents the employee from earning a living in their field.
perturbation.law_explanation: IC 24-1-2-1 generally prohibits contracts that restrain trade. While reasonable non-compete agreements are sometimes enforceable, a clause that prevents an employee from serving customers who actively seek their services, regardless of solicitation, could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if the geographic scope and duration are overly broad.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana General Assembly You need to enable JavaScript to run this app.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a clause that prevents the employee from serving customers who actively seek their services, regardless of solicitation. The Law Explanation states that IC 24-1-2-1 generally prohibits contracts that restrain trade, and the new clause could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, potentially contradicting this law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates the location of the Indiana General Assembly's official site. Although we lack the exact text of IC 24-1-2-1, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation strongly suggest that the modified clause introduces a contradiction, making the non-compete agreement potentially unenforceable due to unreasonable restraint of trade.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted under, subject to and governed by the laws of the State of Indiana, without consideration of the choice of law principles thereof, and all questions concerning its validity, construction and administration shall be determined in accordance with Indiana law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted under, subject to and governed by the laws of the State of California, without consideration of the choice of law principles thereof, and all questions concerning its validity, construction and administration shall be determined in accordance with California law.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text changes the governing law from Indiana to California. This creates a potential conflict because the agreement involves parties based in Indiana, and the services are likely performed in Indiana. Enforcing California law may violate Indiana public policy if it conflicts with Indiana employment laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: While IC 26-1-1-301 addresses choice of law in the context of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC), it establishes principles regarding the enforceability of choice-of-law provisions. Applying California law to an Indiana-based employment relationship could be deemed unenforceable if it contravenes a fundamental policy of Indiana law, especially concerning employee protections or non-compete agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Indiana Code § 26-1-1-301. Choice of Law :: 2022 Indiana Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Indiana Code 2023 Indiana Code 2022 Indiana Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Indiana Code Title 26. Commercial Law Article 1. Uniform Commercial Code Chapter 1. General Provisions 26-1-1-301. Choice of Law Universal Citation: IN Code § 26-1-1-301 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next Sec. 301. (1) Except as otherwise provided in this section, if a transaction bears a reasonable relation to Indiana and also to another state or nation, the parties may agree that the law either of Indiana or of the other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (2) In the absence of an agreement under subsection (1), and except as provided in subsection (3), IC 26-1 applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to Indiana. (3) If any of the following provisions specifies the applicable law, that provision governs, and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (a) IC 26-1-2-402. (b) IC 26-1-2.1-105 and IC 26-1-2.1-106. (c) IC 26-1-4-102. (d) IC 26-1-4.1-507. (e) IC 26-1-5.1-116. (f) IC 26-1-8.1-110. (g) IC 26-1-9.1-301 through IC 26-1-9.1-307. As added by P.L.143-2007, SEC.5. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Indiana may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Indiana law, while the changed contract specifies California law. According to Indiana Code § 26-1-1-301(1), parties can agree on the governing law if the transaction has a reasonable relation to both Indiana and the other state. However, the Perturbation Explanation notes the agreement involves parties based in Indiana and services likely performed there. If applying California law contravenes a fundamental policy of Indiana law (as stated in the Law Explanation), this creates an enforceable contradiction. The provided law snippet supports that there are conditions under which a different jurisdiction can be chosen, but that it can be challenged.
